elements.sent-2411-001: Graph-level representations (and clustering/classification based on these representations) are required in a variety of applications.
elements.sent-2411-002: Examples include identifying malicious network traffic, prediction of protein properties, and many others.
elements.sent-2411-003: Often, data has to stay in isolated local systems (i.e., cannot be centrally shared for analysis) due to a variety of considerations like privacy concerns, lack of trust between the parties, regulations, or simply because the data is too large to be shared sufficiently quickly.
elements.sent-2411-004: This points to the need for federated learning for graph-level representations, a topic that has not been explored much, especially in an unsupervised setting.
elements.sent-2411-005: Addressing this problem, this paper presents a new framework we refer to as Federated Contrastive Learning of Graph-level Representations (FCLG).
elements.sent-2411-006: As the name suggests, our approach builds on contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-007: However, what is unique is that we apply contrastive learning at two levels.
elements.sent-2411-008: The first application is for local unsupervised learning of graph representations.
elements.sent-2411-009: The second level is to address the challenge associated with data distribution variation (i.e. the ``Non-IID issue") when combining local models.
elements.sent-2411-010: Through extensive experiments on the downstream task of graph-level clustering, we demonstrate FCLG outperforms baselines (which apply existing federated methods on existing graph-level clustering methods) with significant margins.
elements.sent-2411-011: There has also been a growing interest in {\em graph-level representation learning , where the goal is to learn embedding of an entire graph.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This problem has numerous applications, such as traffic networks analytics ~\cite{pang2021_cgnn,Xing2020_traffic_GCN , proteins structure studies ~\cite{yang2018_protein_emb,Glig2021_protein_gcn , molecular property prediction ~\cite{Wale2006_chemical_class,wu2018moleculenet , drug discovery ~\cite{Gaudelet2021_drug_discovery,Jiang2021_gnn_drug , sensor networks~\cite{GarciaGarcia2019_TactileGCNAG , and others.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Alternatively, in some cases, data is too large to be shared, or at least cannot be shared sufficiently quickly to provide timely analysis results~\cite{Toyotaro2019_fedfinancial,wu2021_fedgnn,zhang2021_subgraph,ke2021_federated .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Such scenarios have led to interest within the graph analytics community in the well-known paradigm of {\em Federated Learning ~\cite{McMahan2017_FedAvg .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: As a background, federated learning involves training centralized models while the data remains decentralized -- but by sharing and updating model parameters~\cite{Xie2021_FederatedGC .}
elements.sent-2411-016: As specific examples from graph analytics where this approach is required, drug discovery relies on analysis of molecular structures (graphs).
elements.sent-2411-017: However, industry or pharmaceutical research institutes cannot disclose their private data due to the regulatory framework they operate under\cite{Xiong2022_drugdiscovery .}
elements.sent-2411-018: As another example, attributed graphs built on communication between different end-points within a (sub-)network has been used to detect and classify malware~\cite{busch2021nf .}
elements.sent-2411-019: While it is important to consolidate models across multiple networks or administrative domains, sharing the graphs in a timely fashion is impossible.
elements.sent-2411-020: This paper focuses on {\em unsupervised federated learning.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Unsupervised learning is an alternative to supervised learning, which in general, and more specifically in the context of graphs, requires labelling that ranges from being expensive and/or time consuming to simply being impossible~\cite{sun2020_infograph .}
elements.sent-2411-022: For example, one typical way of generating labels for chemical structures is performing the time-consuming Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations~\cite{parr_1983_density .}
elements.sent-2411-023: As a background, to address the challenges associated with attributed graphs, researchers have built on the success of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs)~\cite{kipf2017semi_GCN .}
elements.sent-2411-024: GNN based models have achieved advanced performance on {\em graph-level representation learning, especially in a supervised manner~\cite{xu2018_how_powerful_GNN,Zhang2018_graph_class_gcn,Ying_2018_NIPS_HGRL,pang2021_cgnn,GarciaGarcia2019_TactileGCNAG,Jiang2021_gnn_drug,Xing2020_traffic_GCN,Glig2021_protein_gcn .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: There has also been an interest in unsupervised learning approaches for graph-level representations~\cite{narayanan_2017_graph2vec,hu_2019_strategies,sun2020_infograph , though for the cases when the data is centrally available.}
elements.sent-2411-026: This includes the works based on {\em Graph Kernels ~\cite{prvzulj_2007_biological,orsini_2015_graph and limited GNN based works~\cite{sun2020_infograph,icml2020_Multi-view_CL .}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The proposed method is inspired by the recent success of Contrastive Learning (CL) in various unsupervised learning tasks~\cite{chen_2020_simple_CL,sun2020_infograph,mnih_2013_learning .}
elements.sent-2411-028: To illustrate the type of challenges we need to address -- it is well known that Federated Learning (FL) performance is hindered by the ``Non-IID" (Independent and Identical Distribution) issue, i.e. data at different sites follows different distributions~\cite{Xie2021_FederatedGC .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Thus, both the local models and the global model generated simply by averaging parameters of these models may be far from the global optima \cite{li2021_model_CFL .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In fact, the standard FL algorithm, FedAvg~\cite{McMahan2017_FedAvg might simply not be effective when dealing with a set of heterogeneous graphs~\cite{Xie2021_FederatedGC .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Previous work~\cite{Xie2021_FederatedGC,chen_2020_fedbe,zhang2020_FURL has studied the impact of this Non-IID property especially in supervised learning where label information is explicitly utilized during training.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In such scenarios, skewed label distribution on each client is encoded into the respective local model.
elements.sent-2411-033: Addressing this problem, we propose Federated Contrastive Learning on Graphs (FCLG), a federated graph embedding method with a novel two-level contrastive learning mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-034: Specifically, the two levels in our approach are: 1) {\em intra-contrasting during local training on each client or site, specifically where we contrast augmented views of encoded graph-level representations to make each individual graph sample more distinguishable from others; 2) {\em inter-contrasting between local and global models, ensuring that the global model captures the common patterns underlying graphs from different clients, to achieve better generalization performance than any local model.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: We also show how contrastive learning can be seen as an improvement over the approaches derived from the area of Knowledge Distillation (KD)~\cite{lin2020_ensemble_distillation,kim2021_compare .}
elements.sent-2411-036: The core contributions of this work can be summarized as:.
elements.sent-2411-037: To our best knowledge, FCLG is the first framework to learn graph-level representations in an unsupervised manner under federated settings.
elements.sent-2411-038: FCLG introduces a novel two-level contrastive mechanism to alleviate issues caused by the non-IID situations that arise in federated learning.
elements.sent-2411-039: Through comprehensive empirical studies using four public benchmark datasets for graph-level representation learning, we show that FCLG consistently outperforms other baselines in the downstream graph-level clustering task.
elements.sent-2411-040: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section~\ref{sec: related work introduces related work in federated learning and unsupervised graph-level representation learning.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Section~\ref{sec: method describes the architecture of FCLG and the two-level contrastive mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Section~\ref{sec: experiment , by extensive experiments, evaluates the quality of graph embedding from FCLG for downstream graph-level clustering tasks and demonstrates superior performance over other federated learning baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Section~\ref{sec: empirical provides an empirical study on several important factors affecting FCLG performance.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Section~\ref{sec: conclusion summarizes our conclusions from this research.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Graph-level representation learning aims to predict graph-level labels based on node features and graph structural information for an entire graph.
elements.sent-2411-046: In recent years, GNN based models have achieved great success in supervised graph-level learning, i.e., on the task of graph classification \cite{xu2018_how_powerful_GNN,Zhang2018_graph_class_gcn,Ying_2018_NIPS_HGRL,GarciaGarcia2019_TactileGCNAG,Xing2020_traffic_GCN,Glig2021_protein_gcn .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Considering unsupervised graph-level learning, early studies established models mainly based on graph kernels that used ``hand-crafted" similarity measures between substructures~\cite{NIPS2009_subtree,JMLR2010_graph_kernels,Yanardag2015_DGK,Yang2018_structural_Conv .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Recent GNN based models~\cite{sun2020_infograph,icml2020_Multi-view_CL gained state-of-the-art results by applying unsupervised contrastive learning mechanism as described below.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Contrastive learning based methods learn discriminative representations by reducing the distance between positive sample pairs, while enlarging the distance between negative sample pairs.
elements.sent-2411-050: These approaches have achieved excellent performance in visual representation learning~\cite{hjelm_2019_DIM,Tian_multi_coding_2020,chen_2020_simple_CL,Sohn_N-pair_loss_objective_2016,contrastiveLoss_wu2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: SimCLR~\cite{chen_2020_simple_CL proposed a simple yet powerful instance-wise contrastive objective to differentiate each data instance from others for image classification.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Recently, the contrastive principle has been applied to establish GNN models for graph-level representation learning~\cite{qiu2020_GCC,icml2020_Multi-view_CL,sun2020_infograph .}
elements.sent-2411-053: Federated learning (FL) is a distributed learning paradigm that can train centralized models on decentralized data.
elements.sent-2411-054: A detailed discussion of FL is beyond the scope of this paper, but we discuss a few closely related efforts.
elements.sent-2411-055: In recent years, FL has been studied in the context of visual representation learning~\cite{chen_2020_fedbe,li2021_model_CFL,zhang2020_FURL,zhuang2021collaborative,he2021fedcv .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Aligned with our interest in using contrastive learning, Li {\em et al. ~\cite{li2021_model_CFL proposed a model-level contrastive learning approach aiming to alleviate non-IID issues and achieved advanced image classification results.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: However, FL studies on graph-level representations remain limited and have so far focused on supervised learning ~\cite{Xie2021_FederatedGC,he2021_spreadgnn .}
elements.sent-2411-058: Suppose there are $K$ separate clients $C_{1 , C_{2 ,\cdots, C_{K}$, where client $C_{i}$ has a local graph dataset $S^{i}$ containing a set of graphs.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Each graph can be represented as $G = (\mathcal{V , \mathcal{E , X)$, where $\mathcal{V}$ is the set of $n$ vertices, $\mathcal{E}$ is the edge set, $X \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times F}$ is the feature matrix where each node is associated with a $F$-dimensional feature vector.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Let $A$ be the $n \times n$ adjacency matrix capturing all edges in the graph $G$.
elements.sent-2411-061: For simplicity, we can also denote each graph as $G(A, X)$.
elements.sent-2411-062: Our objective is to learn a model $ \psi_{\omega : G \to \mathbb{R ^{d $, where $\omega$ is the corresponding parameter set.}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: More specifically, we have a federated learning problem where our goal is to learn a parameter set $\omega_{g}$ using the datasets $S \triangleq \bigcup_{i \in [K] S^{i $.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The individual parameter set at the client $C_i$ is denoted as $\omega_{i}$.
elements.sent-2411-065: In other words, the $d$-dimensional graph-level embedding $ \psi_{\omega (G)$ must be learnt collaboratively from a set of clients, each of which stores a distinct set of graphs, while inter-client raw graph data transfer is disallowed.}
elements.sent-2411-066: As additional background, we introduce the first basic FL algorithm FedAvg~\cite{McMahan2017_FedAvg which is commonly used as the starting point for developing more advanced FL frameworks~\cite{Xie2021_FederatedGC .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: In FedAvg, the global model on the server will aggregate local model parameters transmitted from clients and distribute the aggregated parameters back to all clients.
elements.sent-2411-068: During each communication round, each client downloads the model from the server $\omega_{i \gets \omega_{g ^{(t)}$ and trains the model $\omega_{i}$ with its own data $S^{i}$ for $E_{local}$ epochs.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Each client $S^{i}$ will send locally updated parameters $\omega_{i ^{(t)}$ to the server, then the global model $\omega_{g ^{(t+1)}$ will be updated by a simple weighted average: \begin{equation}\label{eq:FedAvg_loss} \begin{split} \omega_{g}^{(t+1)} = \sum_{i=1}^{K}\frac{|S^{i}|}{|S|}\omega_{i}^{(t)} \end{split}\end{equation} where $|S^{i |$ denotes the size of data on the client $S^{i}$ and $|S |$ is the total size of all data samples distributed over all clients.}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: The server will broadcast newly updated parameters $\omega_{g ^{(t+1)}$ to clients for the next round of training.}
elements.sent-2411-071: To solve the problem we have posed, i.e., federated learning of a graph-level representation in an unsupervised fashion, we can build on existing frameworks like InfoGraph~\cite{sun2020_infograph and then apply an idea like FedAvg described above.}
elements.sent-2411-072: However, as we will demonstrate in the experimental evaluation, such combination produces subpar results.
elements.sent-2411-073: To provide background on our approach, we use the following formalism.
elements.sent-2411-074: Let us say we have a local model $f_{c}$ from a client and the global model $f_{s}$ on the server.
elements.sent-2411-075: On the server, we wish $f_{s}$ to encode all samples into a representation where each sample is more distinguishable from others.
elements.sent-2411-076: In other words, the overall model training will push apart $f_{s (x_{i )$ and $f_{s (x_{j )$, where different graph samples $x_{i}$, $x_{j}$ can be from different clients.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: However, such a goal may not be achieved by solely aggregating local models since a skewed data distribution can be potentially encoded inside each local model $f_{c}$.
elements.sent-2411-078: One way of viewing federated learning is that we are trying to distill knowledge from multiple {\em teacher models trained on isolated clients into a single global student model on the server.}
elements.sent-2411-079: An earlier federated learning effort~\cite{lin2020_ensemble_distillation has applied a knowledge distillation (KD) technique for model fusion in image classification tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-080: A general objective function of KD (as used by these authors~\cite{lin2020_ensemble_distillation ) is the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence loss between the softened probability distributions of the teacher models and the student model. \begin{equation}\label{eq: KL-loss-KD} l_{KL} \bigg ( \sigma(f_{c}^{t}(x) ), \sigma(f_{s}(x)) \bigg ) \nonumber = \sigma(f_{s}(x)) \cdot \bigg (\log \sigma(f_{s}(x)) - \log \sigma(f_{c}^{t}(x)) \bigg )\end{equation} where $\sigma(\cdot)$ denotes the softmax function.}
elements.sent-2411-081: KL divergence loss has achieved considerable success, which can be attributed to its ability to control the softness of targets via the temperature-scaling hyperparameter $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-082: Specifically, utilizing a larger value makes the softmax vectors smooth over latent classes~\cite{kim2021_compare,furlanello2018_born,tang2020_understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-083: Moving further, it has been observed that the logit matching is positively correlated to the performance improvement in KD~\cite{kim2021_compare .}
elements.sent-2411-084: These authors considered the mean squared error (MSE) between the logit vectors such that the student model can directly learn the logit of the teacher model. \begin{equation}\label{eq: MSE-loss-KD} l_{MSE} \bigg ( f_{c}^{t}(x) , f_{s}(x) \bigg ) = || f_{c}^{t}(x) - f_{s}(x) ||_{2}^{2}\end{equation} For the KD loss functions like KL-divergence in Eq.\ref{eq: KL-loss-KD and MSE in Eq.\ref{eq: MSE-loss-KD , we are pushing closer the representations $f_{s (x_{i )$ and $f_{c (x_{i )$.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq: MSE-loss-KD} l_{MSE} \bigg ( f_{c}^{t}(x) , f_{s}(x) \bigg ) = || f_{c}^{t}(x) - f_{s}(x) ||_{2}^{2}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-085: This alleviates the local drift by aligning the local logits with that of the server model during training.
elements.sent-2411-086: In our work, what we discover is that the paradigm of {\em Contrastive Learning can help significantly improve both unsupervised graph-level representation on individual sites as well as in federated settings in a unified fashion.}
elements.sent-2411-087: From a knowledge distillation perspective, the inter-contrasting mechanism can be taken as an advanced KD, more specifically an ensembling distillation technique. \begin{equation}\label{eq: inter-contrast-KD} l_{inter} = \log \bigg (1 + \exp( f_{c}^{t}(x) \cdot f_{c}^{t-1}(x) - f_{c}^{t}(x) \cdot f_{s}(x) ) \bigg )\end{equation} The idea here is that the local drift will be hindered by using more historical information of local training.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}\label{eq: inter-contrast-KD} l_{inter} = \log \bigg (1 + \exp( f_{c}^{t}(x) \cdot f_{c}^{t-1}(x) - f_{c}^{t}(x) \cdot f_{s}(x) ) \bigg )\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-088: We not only try to push closer the in-situ local model $f_{c ^{t (x) $ and $f_{s (x)$, but also aim to push apart the current representations $f_{c ^{t (x) $ away from its previous round $ f_{c ^{t-1 (x) $.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Prior graph-level learning approaches, such as InfoGraph, ~\cite{sun2020_infograph,icml2020_Multi-view_CL are based on mutual information maximization between multi-scale graph structures like graph-level and sub-graph or node-level representations.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Specifically, they consider the summarized patch representation centered at each node as a {\em positive example .}
elements.sent-2411-091: This positive example is compared with the global representation of the entire graph, based on mutual information.
elements.sent-2411-092: There are also negative samples that arise from {\em fake graphs' local representations.}
elements.sent-2411-093: In this paper, we produce positive samples of a given graph by {\em randomized augmentations -- a powerful and proven approach to produce similar pairs for deep learning models ~\cite{qiu2020_GCC that has not yet been fully used for learning graph-level representations.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Basically, we create augmented similar and dissimilar graphs and then apply an instance-wise contrasting objective~\cite{chen_2020_simple_CL .}
elements.sent-2411-095: Moreover, in federated learning setting, this approach can directly compare graphs available at each client against each other to sharpen the characteristics of each individual graph sample.
elements.sent-2411-096: Thus, one of the novel parts of our approach is that we apply contrastive learning at two levels.
elements.sent-2411-097: The first application ({\em intra-contrasting ) is for local learning of graph representations -- specifically, for contrasting augmented views of graphs within each client as described above.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The second level ({\em inter-contrasting ) is to contrast between the global model from the server and the local model on each client, as already introduced through Equation~\ref{eq: inter-contrast-KD .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: Suppose we are given graph-level representations $U$ of a batch of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-100: After generating the set of augmented views $V$, the contrastive mechanism~\cite{chen_2020_simple_CL works as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-101: We denote $u \in U$ as the representation of a single graph in the view $U$ and $v \in V$ as the augmented counterpart of $u$.
elements.sent-2411-102: Under this contrasting strategy, $v$ forms the only positive sample of $u$ and any other graph representation in these two views, i.e. $U \bigcup V$, is regarded as a negative sample.
elements.sent-2411-103: Thus, we get the contrastive loss: \begin{equation}\label{eq:CL_pairwise_objective} \begin{adjustbox}{max width=0.95\columnwidth} $ L(u,v) = \log \big \{ {\sum_{\substack{z \in U \bigcup V z \neq u} } \exp \big \{ \frac{u \cdot z}{\tau} \big \} }/{ \exp \big \{ \frac{u \cdot v}{\tau} \big \} } \big \} $ \end{adjustbox}\end{equation} where $\tau$ is the temperature parameter~\cite{chen_2020_simple_CL .}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eq:CL_pairwise_objective} \begin{adjustbox}{max width=0.95\columnwidth} $ L(u,v) = \log \big \{ {\sum_{\substack{z \in U \bigcup V z \neq u} } \exp \big \{ \frac{u \cdot z}{\tau} \big \} }/{ \exp \big \{ \frac{u \cdot v}{\tau} \big \} } \big \} $ \end{adjustbox}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-104: Suppose we are training the local model on a batch consisting of $B$ graphs, the overall training loss to be minimized is defined as the average agreement $L$ over all positive pairs as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:CL_loss} \begin{split} l_{intra} = \frac{1}{2B}\sum_{u \in U}[L(u, v) + L(v, u)] \end{split}\end{equation} Intra-contrasting will force the local model to capture robust characteristics behind all the graphs stored on each client.
elements.sent-2411-105: The resulting graph embeddings are expected to make each graph sample more distinguishable from others so as to ease the downstream graph-level clustering task.
elements.sent-2411-106: When there is a skewed or non-identical distribution among the clients, the lost opportunity of contrasting with data samples from other clients can have a significant impact on the generalization performance of the resulting model.
elements.sent-2411-107: This is analogous to how, in federated supervised learning, the local model can explicitly encode the local label distribution and thereby drift away from the global optimum during training.
elements.sent-2411-108: As already stated above, we draw inspiration from a model-level contrasting mechanism proposed by the federated image classification framework proposed in MOON~\cite{li2021_model_CFL .}
elements.sent-2411-109: This work verified that the skewed local data distribution can cause a drift in the local updates leading the local model to learn worse representations than the global model.
elements.sent-2411-110: We extended their idea to the graph learning domain in order to control local training drift and bridge the gap between graph representations learned by the local model and the global model.
elements.sent-2411-111: This, when applied in an unsupervised manner, is the second part of our novel two-level contrasting framework.
elements.sent-2411-112: Rewriting Equation~\ref{eq: inter-contrast-KD in our specific context, our goal is to decrease the distance between $U^{t}$ (representations learned by the current local model) and $U_{s}$ (representations learned by the global model) while increasing the distance between $U^{t}$ and $U_{c ^{t-1}$ (representations learned by the local model with parameters from the previous local epoch).}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Through this process, we pull $U^{t}$ and $U_{c ^{t-1}$ apart to prevent the local model from drifting along skewed subset of data after iterations of local training.}
elements.sent-2411-114: Meanwhile, we push $U^{t}$ and $U_{s}$ closer to keep optimizing the generalization performance of the global model.
elements.sent-2411-115: Specifically we minimize the contrastive loss as: \begin{equation}\label{eq: graph-level CL server and client} \begin{adjustbox}{max width=0.9\columnwidth} $ l_{inter} = \log\frac{\exp(sim(U^{t}, U_{s})/\tau^{\prime})+\exp(sim(U^{t}, U_{c}^{t-1})/\tau^{\prime})}{\exp(sim(U^{t}, U_{s})/\tau^{\prime})} $ \end{adjustbox}\end{equation} where $sim(\cdot , \cdot)$ is a cosine similarity function and $\tau^{\prime}$ is the temperature for inter-contrasting.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{eq: graph-level CL server and client} \begin{adjustbox}{max width=0.9\columnwidth} $ l_{inter} = \log\frac{\exp(sim(U^{t}, U_{s})/\tau^{\prime})+\exp(sim(U^{t}, U_{c}^{t-1})/\tau^{\prime})}{\exp(sim(U^{t}, U_{s})/\tau^{\prime})} $ \end{adjustbox}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-116: Next, we also construct a model variant by contrasting node-level representations $H$ in place of graph-level representations $U$ by minimizing: \begin{equation}\label{eq: node-level CL server and client} \begin{adjustbox}{max width=0.9\columnwidth} $ l^{H}_{inter} = \log\frac{exp(sim(H^{t}, H_{s})/\tau^{\prime})+exp(sim(H^{t}, H_{c}^{t-1})/\tau^{\prime})}{exp(sim(H^{t}, H_{s})/\tau^{\prime})} $ \end{adjustbox}\end{equation} where $H^{t}$, $H_{c ^{t-1}$, and $H_{s}$ are the node-level counterpart representations of $U^{t}$, $U_{c ^{t-1}$, and $ U_{s}$, respectively.}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\label{eq: node-level CL server and client} \begin{adjustbox}{max width=0.9\columnwidth} $ l^{H}_{inter} = \log\frac{exp(sim(H^{t}, H_{s})/\tau^{\prime})+exp(sim(H^{t}, H_{c}^{t-1})/\tau^{\prime})}{exp(sim(H^{t}, H_{s})/\tau^{\prime})} $ \end{adjustbox}\end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\columnwidth]{Figure/arch_FCLG.pdf} \caption{Schematic of FCLG framework architecture. Given one batch of graph data $G_{b}(X, A)$, graph diffusion is used to generate an augmented counterpart $G_{d}(X, A)$. These two views are fed to the subsequent GIN based encoder $f$ to generate latent node-level representations ($H_{c}^{t}$ and $\widehat{H}_{c}^{t}$, respectively). The graph pooling function $g$ will further summarize ($H_{c}^{t}$, $ \widehat{H}_{c}^{t}$) by summing up all features for each graph along the columns, yielding $(U^{t},V^{t})$, on which intra-contrastive learning is performed (the superscript $t$ indicates the local training epoch). The inter-contrastive learning step will pull apart the representations ($U_{c}^{t-1}$, $U^{t}$) between adjacent local training epochs on each client, and bring closer ($U^{t}, U_{s}$) to improve the generalization performance of $U_{s}$. } \label{fig:FCLG-arch_two_level}
elements.sent-2411-117: Based on the two-level contrastive learning idea, our framework first learns a powerful graph embedding in a self-supervised manner from decentralized data.
elements.sent-2411-118: The resulting embedding can then be used for downstream graph-level clustering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-119: Figure~\ref{fig:FCLG-arch_two_level presents an overview of FCLG architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Given a batch of input graph data $G_{b (X, A)$, a graph diffusion precomputing procedure~\cite{klicpera_2019_diffusion is performed to generate the augmentation view $G_{d (X, A)$ (following~\cite{icml2020_Multi-view_CL ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: The subsequent graph embedding generation module comprises a GIN~\cite{xu2018_how_powerful_GNN based encoder $f$.}
elements.sent-2411-122: This encoder $f$ involves a set of parameters denoted by $\omega$ and a non-parametric graph pooling function $g$.
elements.sent-2411-123: We use the GIN~\cite{xu2018_how_powerful_GNN due to its advanced performance in graph-level tasks~\cite{Xie2021_FederatedGC and apply a summing-up graph pooling function as described in~\cite{icml2020_Multi-view_CL,sun2020_infograph,Ying_2018_NIPS_HGRL,Zhang2018_graph_class_gcn .}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: Within this module, $f$ containing stacked $L$-layers of GIN, will first abstract information from graphs into a series of latent representations $ \{h^{k \}_{k=1 ^{L $.}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Inspired by~\cite{xu_2018_representation , we summarize representations at all depths of neural networks through concatenation along the feature dimension, with the goal of capturing local information centered at every node: \begin{equation}\begin{adjustbox}{max width=0.95\columnwidth} $ H = CONCAT(\{h^{k} \}_{k=1}^{L}), \quad U = POOL( H ) \nonumber $ \end{adjustbox}\end{equation} Here, $ H $ indicates the node-level latent representations and $U$ h is the pooled graph-level representation of the input graph $G_b(X, A)$.}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\begin{adjustbox}{max width=0.95\columnwidth} $ H = CONCAT(\{h^{k} \}_{k=1}^{L}), \quad U = POOL( H ) \nonumber $ \end{adjustbox}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-126: Similarly, $V$ is the pooled graph-level representation of the augmented view $G_d(X, A)$.
elements.sent-2411-127: The model will be optimized in a self-supervised manner via contrastive learning objectives.. Complete Algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-128: The entire training process is summarized in Algorithm \ref{alg:FCLG Train .}
elements.sent-2411-129: During each communication round, the global model on the server will send model parameters to clients, receive the local model from each client, and update the global model by Eq.\ref{eq:FedAvg_loss (on line 2-9).}
elements.sent-2411-130: During local training, each client $C_i$ first downloads the most updated model parameters from the server (on line 11).
elements.sent-2411-131: Next, we perform the training on each batch of graphs $G_{b}$ locally stored on each client.
elements.sent-2411-132: Augmented view of graphs $G_{d}$ will be generated through graph diffusion mechanism summarized earlier (on line 14).
elements.sent-2411-133: The GIN~\cite{xu2018_how_powerful_GNN based encoder $f$ and graph pooling function $g$ abstract graph data into latent graph-level representations (on line 15-16): 1) $U^{t}$ by the current local model $w_{i ^t$ encoding $G_{b}$; 2) $V^{t}$ by the current local model $w_{i ^t$ encoding the augmented view $G_{d}$; 3) $U_{s}$ by the global model $w^{j}$ encoding $G_{b}$; 4) $U_{c ^{t-1}$ by the previous epoch of local model $w_{i ^{t-1}$ encoding $G_{b}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: These obtained representations will be used to compute intra-contrasting and inter-contrasting losses (on line 17).
elements.sent-2411-135: The total training loss can be computed by summing two contrastive losses up (on line 18) by: \begin{equation}\label{eq:FCLG total loss} \begin{adjustbox}{max width=0.9\columnwidth} $ l_{tot} = l_{inter} + l_{intra} $ \end{adjustbox}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\label{eq:FCLG total loss} \begin{adjustbox}{max width=0.9\columnwidth} $ l_{tot} = l_{inter} + l_{intra} $ \end{adjustbox}\end{equation}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{FCLG training for graph-level representations} \label{alg:FCLG Train} \begin{itemize} \Statex{Input: local training epochs $E$, communication rounds $T$, number of clients $K$ and $\gamma$ the fraction of clients participating in each round. } \Statex{Output: learned model parameters $\omega^{T}$ } \end{itemize} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State{Server:} \State{initialize $\omega^{0}$} \For{$j = 0, 1, \cdots, T-1 $} \State{Randomly sample a subset of $K_{\gamma}$ clients} \For{$ i = 1, 2, \cdots, K_{\gamma}$ in parallel} \State{send global model $\omega^{j}$ to $C_{i}$} \State{$\omega_{i}^{j} \gets $ \textbf{Client($i, \omega^{j}$)}} \EndFor \State{$\omega^{j+1} \gets \sum^{K_{\gamma}}_{i}\frac{|S^{i}|}{|S|}\omega^{j}_{i}$} \EndFor \State{return $\omega^{T}$} \State{\textbf{Client($i, \omega^{j}$):}} \State{$\omega^{0}_{i} \gets \omega^{j}$} \For{$t = 1, 2, \cdots, E $} \For{each batch of graphs $ G_{b} \in S^{i}$} \State{$G_{d} \gets $ graph diffusion on $G_{b}$} \State{$U^{t} \gets g(f_{\omega^{t}_{i}}(G_{b}))$; $V^{t} \gets g( f_{\omega^{t}_{i}}(G_{d}) )$} \State{ $U_{s} \gets g(f_{\omega^{j}}(G_{b})) $; $U_{c}^{t-1} \gets g(f_{\omega_{i}^{t-1}}(G_{b})) $} \State{Calculate $l_{intra} $ from Eq.\ref{eq:CL_loss} and $l_{inter}$ from Eq.\ref{eq: graph-level CL server and client}} \State{$l_{tot} \gets l_{inter} + l_{intra} $ } \State{SGD update on $\omega^{t}_{i}$} \EndFor \EndFor \State{return $\omega^{E}_{i}$} \end{algorithmic}
elements.figure-2411-002: \caption{Statistics of datasets for unsupervised graph-level representation learning. } \begin{adjustbox}{max width=\columnwidth} \begin{tabular}{llrrrr} Domain Dataset Graphs Avg. Nodes Avg. Edges class Proteins ENZYMES \cite{Karsten2005_protein_graph_kernels} $ 600 $ $ 32.63 $ $ 62.14 $ $ 6 $ PROTEINS \cite{Karsten2005_protein_graph_kernels} $ 1113 $ $ 39.06 $ $ 72.82 $ $ 2 $ Molecules DHFR \cite{Sutherland2003_spline} $ 467 $ $ 42.43 $ $ 44.54 $ $ 2 $ NCI1 \cite{Wale2008_chemical} $ 4110 $ $ 29.87 $ $ 32.30 $ $ 2 $ \end{tabular} \end{adjustbox} \label{tab: graph representation dataset}
elements.sent-2411-136: Datasets: we use four datasets~\cite{morris2020_tudataset for the graph-level clustering task covering multiple domains including: two protein datasets (ENZYMES, PROTEINS)~\cite{Karsten2005_protein_graph_kernels and two molecule datasets (DHFR~\cite{Sutherland2003_spline and NCI1~\cite{Wale2008_chemical ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: Each of these datasets comprises a set of graphs with binary or multiple class labels.
elements.sent-2411-138: More information about these datasets is presented in Table \ref{tab: graph representation dataset .}
elements.sent-2411-139: We divide graphs of each dataset into 6 clients for federated training.
elements.sent-2411-140: Parameter Settings: Our FCLG framework is implemented in PyTorch 1.7 on CUDA 10.1, and we use the graph diffusion implementation from Pytorch Geometric~\cite{PyGeom_Fey_Lenssen_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-141: Our experiments are performed on nodes with a dual Intel Xeon 6148s @2.4GHz CPU and dual NVIDIA Volta V100 w/ 16GB memory GPU and 384 GB DDR4 memory.
elements.sent-2411-142: We applied a AdamW optimization method with a decoupled weight decay regularization technique \cite{loshchilov2019decoupled_weight_decay .}
elements.sent-2411-143: The detailed hyper-parameter settings are included in the Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-144: All baselines are implemented with their officially released codes as listed in the Appendix and we follow their basic settings with all hyper-parameters tuned for optimal clustering performance on each dataset.
elements.sent-2411-145: We use the KMeans implementation from scikit-learn \cite{scikit-learn .}
elements.sent-2411-146: Baselines: Because our work is the first to do unsupervised graph-level representation learning in a federated setting we had to construct our own comparison baselines.
elements.sent-2411-147: To this end, we combined existing unsupervised graph-level representation methods together with popular federated schemes.
elements.sent-2411-148: Specifically, for the former, we used InfoGraph that maximizes the mutual information between the graph-level representation and the representations of substructures of different scales~\cite{sun2020_infograph ; MVGRL, which involves much of the same idea but contrasts node representations from one view with graph representation of another view~\cite{icml2020_Multi-view_CL .}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Following similar settings from recent efforts~\cite{Xie2021_FederatedGC,zhang2020_FURL , we combine different prior federated learning strategies FedAvg and FedProx with the aforementioned graph-level representation learning methods as our baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Here FedAvg~\cite{McMahan2017_FedAvg aggregates the updated model parameters from local clients and updates the global model by taking average weighted by the dataset size as in Eq.\ref{eq:FedAvg_loss .}}
elements.sent-2411-151: FedProx~\cite{li2020federated_prox , on top of FedAvg, introduces an additional proximal term into training objective to correct the local training updates.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Metrics: We evaluate the quality of learned graph-level embeddings through its performance on the downstream graph-level clustering, specifically using two metrics: accuracy and macro F1-score to measure the accordance between prediction and ground-truth labels.
elements.sent-2411-153: To quantify non-identical distributions for different (versions of) datasets, we apply an average Earthmover's Distance (EMD)~\cite{EMD_rubner1998metric metric.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Following~\cite{hsu_2020_federated , we take the discrete graph class distribution $ \bf{q_{i $ for each client.}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: The class distribution across the total population including datasets from all clients is denoted as $\bf{p}$.
elements.sent-2411-156: Hence, the non-identical distribution of a dataset is calculated as average distances between the clients and the population in a weighted fashion: \begin{equation}\begin{adjustbox}{max width=0.95\columnwidth} $ \sum_i\frac{|S_{i}|}{|S|}Dist(\bf{q_{i}}, \bf{p}) \nonumber $ \end{adjustbox}\end{equation} As indicated earlier, $|S_{i |$ indicates the data size on each client and $|S|=\sum_{i |S_{i |$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Dist$(\cdot,\cdot)$ is a specific distance metric, where in our particular case we use the EMD$(\bf{q , \bf{p ) \triangleq ||\bf{q - \bf{p ||_{1}$ and EMD$(\bf{q , \bf{p ) \in [0, 2]$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-158: Hence a larger EMD implies a more skewed distribution.

elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{subfigure}{0.33\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure/expr/T-SNE_FCLG-Proteins/single_server_1_tSNE.pdf} \caption{Communication Round 1} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure/expr/T-SNE_FCLG-Proteins/single_server_10_tSNE.pdf} \caption{Communication Round 10} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.32\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Figure/expr/T-SNE_FCLG-Proteins/single_server_20_tSNE.pdf} \caption{Communication Round 20} \end{subfigure} \caption{T-SNE for graph-level embeddings of PROTEINS at different communication round during FCLG training. Each color represents a distinct class.} \label{fig: FCLG PROTEINS tsne embedding model}
elements.sent-2411-159: We evaluate the clustering performance following the clustering evaluation protocol as taken by previous works \cite{icml2020_Multi-view_CL .}
elements.sent-2411-160: Specifically, we set the number of clusters to the number of ground-truth graph-level classes and perform K-Means algorithm \cite{Kmeans_1979 on the resulting graph-level embedding.}
elements.sent-2411-161: We design two settings based on the data distribution across clients: 1) Non-IID where the graph data distribution over clients will be skewed away from $\bf{p}$, specific EMD values will vary across different data; 2) IID where each client will follow approximately the same population distribution $\bf{p}$ with EMD values close to zero.
elements.sent-2411-162: Table~\ref{tab: Non-IID graph-level clustering shows the graph-level clustering results on four datasets with non-IID client data -- each experiment is performed for 10 times with both the average and the range reported.}
elements.sent-2411-163: We provide the EMD values in alignment with each dataset name.
elements.sent-2411-164: Besides FCLG, we also tested the variant of FCLG (FCLG-H), where intra-contrasting is conducted between node-level representations $H$ via Eq.~\ref{eq: node-level CL server and client .}
elements.sent-2411-165: Our FCLG based models obtained state-of-the-art graph-level clustering metrics across all datasets.
elements.sent-2411-166: Specifically, FCLG achieved a significant clustering gain over the closest baseline: 7\% on PROTEINS, around 5\% on ENZYMES and DHFR, and 2\% on NCI1.
elements.sent-2411-167: Analyzing further, Figure~\ref{fig:FCLG-acc_commu shows graph-level clustering accuracy after a varying number of communication rounds on the dataset PROTEINS.}
elements.sent-2411-168: As can be seen, FCLG starts achieving a clearly superior performance after a few rounds.
elements.sent-2411-169: The variant we have developed, FCLG-H, uses node-level representations for inter-contrasting, hence will include more detailed information (and more noise) compared with FCLG.
elements.sent-2411-170: The influence of this appears to be limited since similar clustering performance is observed between FCLG and FCLG-H.
elements.sent-2411-171: Therefore, we can conclude that FCLG based models show robustness against the scale of graph representations (node-level or graph-level) during inter-contrasting.
elements.sent-2411-172: In looking across baselines, we can see MVGRL based models generally have worse performance.
elements.sent-2411-173: Although MVGRL based models achieve higher accuracy than InfoGraph on DHFR, their macro F1-scores are less consistent.
elements.sent-2411-174: Depending on the neural network applied (i.e. InfoGraph or MVGRL), the different federated strategies (FedAvg and FedProx) have different impacts on clustering performance.
elements.sent-2411-175: For MVGRL, both federated strategies yield fairly close clustering results.
elements.sent-2411-176: For InfoGraph, InfoGraph-FedProx generally performed better (by 1-2\%) than InfoGraph-FedAvg, for ENZYMES, DHFR, and NCI1.
elements.sent-2411-177: To investigate the effect of different ensembling distillation strategies, we construct multiple model variants on top of FCLG:.
elements.sent-2411-178: Intra-FedAvg by removing the term $l_{inter}$ (Eq.\ref{eq:FCLG total loss ) and only keeping intra-contrastive loss;.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Intra-KL by replacing $l_{inter}$ with $l_{KL}$ as in Eq.\ref{eq: KL-loss-KD ;.}
elements.sent-2411-180: Intra-MSE by replacing $l_{inter}$ with $l_{MSE}$ as in Eq.\ref{eq: MSE-loss-KD .}
elements.sent-2411-181: Our tables~\ref{tab: Non-IID graph-level clustering ~\ref{tab: IID graph-level clustering show results from these versions as well.}}
elements.sent-2411-182: First, considering Table~\ref{tab: Non-IID graph-level clustering , Several observations can be made.}
elements.sent-2411-183: Comparing against the baselines, we can see that our novel intra-contrastive learning method is providing significant improvements over the existing centralized baseline approaches.
elements.sent-2411-184: Compared with the corresponding FCLG performance, removing inter-contrasting as in Intra-FedAvg can cause a performance drop in two of the four datasets, specifically, nearly 5\% for DHFR and 2\% for ENZYMES.
elements.sent-2411-185: For PROTEINS and NCI1, no obvious change has been observed which indicates that intra-contrasting with averaging itself can yield a robust enough model against local model drifts for certain datasets.
elements.sent-2411-186: In comparison with the other models Intra-KL and Intra-MSE, FCLG presents significant advantages, especially in datasets PROTEINS by 3\% and DHFR by 5\%.
elements.sent-2411-187: That is because the KD loss function $l_{inter}$ utilizes richer information (previous round of local model parameters) than either $l_{KL}$ or $l_{MSE}$.
elements.sent-2411-188: Table~\ref{tab: IID graph-level clustering covers the graph-level clustering results in one special case where all clients data approximately follow the same distribution (i.e. EMD values close to zero).}
elements.sent-2411-189: FCLG and FCLG-H continue to outperform the baselines, which in this case shows the benefits of our intra-contrasting approach.
elements.sent-2411-190: As in previous experiments, the gains are substantial.
elements.sent-2411-191: Similar to the observation from the non-IID condition, FCLG has maintained non-trivial advantages over other model variants (Intra-FedAvg, Intra-KL, Intra-MSE) with various distillation techniques.
elements.sent-2411-192: When compared with non-IID situations, FCLG based models achieve better clustering performance for PROTEINS, are a bit worse metrics for ENZYMES and DHFR, and show similar results for NCI1.
elements.sent-2411-193: InfoGraph-FedAvg shows consistently better performance in IID situations than in non-IID ones; however, such consistent trend is not observed in all the other baselines.
elements.sent-2411-194: Overall, the non-identical distribution effect varies across different combination of models and datasets and a skewed distribution can yield better or worse performance.
elements.sent-2411-195: This will be further illustrated by Figure~\ref{fig:FCLG-acc_EMD as discussed later.}
elements.sent-2411-196: Figure \ref{fig: FCLG PROTEINS tsne embedding model shows the comparison between graph-level embeddings at the different stages of training using t-SNE algorithm \cite{t-SNE_algo .}}
elements.sent-2411-197: We can see that graph-level embedding as obtained from FCLG model can gradually recognize graph-level semantic distributions (i.e. the overlap reduces between the classes).
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{subfigure}{\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{Figure/expr/ACC_vs_EMD.pdf} \caption{Accuracy Vs. varying skew of data (fixed number -- 6 clients).} \label{fig:FCLG-acc_EMD} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{\columnwidth} \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{Figure/expr/ACC_vs_client_num.pdf} \caption{Accuracy Vs. varying number of clients (common EMD value of 0.58).} \label{fig:FCLG-acc_client_num} \end{subfigure} \caption{Accuracy Vs. varying data distribution on PROTEINS, each result is obtained by fine-tuning hyper-parameters separately .} \label{fig: FCLG accuracy data distribution}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{Figure/expr/accuracy_vs_local_epoch_num.pdf} \caption{FCLG Accuracy Vs. No. of Local Train Epoch on PROTEINS (20 communication rounds). Experiments utilize the same Non-IID settings as in Table~\ref{tab: Non-IID graph-level clustering}. } \label{fig:FCLG-acc_local_train_epoch}
elements.sent-2411-198: In this section, we study two important factors on FCLG's learning power: data distribution across clients, number of local training epochs.
elements.sent-2411-199: Unless explicitly illustrated, experiments for empirical analysis utilize the same Non-IID settings in Table~\ref{tab: Non-IID graph-level clustering as the default settings and report the converged results averaged from 10 runs.}
elements.sent-2411-200: Figure~\ref{fig: FCLG accuracy data distribution shows how the graph-level clustering accuracy changes with different data distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-201: Specifically, we include two separate experiments: 1) we change data skew while the number of clients is fixed -- Figure~\ref{fig:FCLG-acc_EMD ; 2) we vary the number of clients while the skew (EMD value) is fixed -- Figure~\ref{fig:FCLG-acc_client_num .}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Increasing EMD tends to magnify the Non-IID impact while more number of clients can restrict amount of information accessible to each local model.
elements.sent-2411-203: In both cases, FCLG holds performance superiority over all other baselines and shows good robustness to both distribution skew and the number of clients.
elements.sent-2411-204: As expected, FCLG's relative performance over baselines is even better with higher EMD value (Figure~\ref{fig:FCLG-acc_EMD ).}
elements.sent-2411-205: Comparing among the baselines, non-identical distributions do not have a consistent impact on the performance of these baselines and no clear patterns were observed.
elements.sent-2411-206: In particular, MVGRL based models mostly stay constant for different EMD values.
elements.sent-2411-207: This observation is different from those made by earlier supervised federated learning effort~\cite{Xie2021_FederatedGC,chen_2020_fedbe,zhang2020_FURL , where increasing skew was shown to negatively impact the performance more severely.}
elements.sent-2411-208: This may be because information of labels is not explicitly encoded into models during unsupervised training and thus side effects of skewed graph-level label distribution are lower.
elements.subsection-2411-010: Impact of Number of Local Training Epochs
elements.sent-2411-209: We investigate the influence of number of local training epochs on graph-level clustering accuracy as in Figure~\ref{fig:FCLG-acc_local_train_epoch .}
elements.sent-2411-210: It should be noted that inter-contrasting will not take effect when the local train epoch number is 1.
elements.sent-2411-211: This is because no previous local training model parameters are available for contrasting.
elements.sent-2411-212: Though different datasets show different behaviors, we can observe the following patterns: 1) very small number of local train epochs may yield slow optimization process resulting in low clustering accuracy; 2) when the number of local epochs gets very high on the other hand, local drift may start to dominate and negatively impact the generalization performance.
elements.sent-2411-213: This seems like the case of two of the datasets, i.e., PROTEINS and ENZYMES.
elements.sent-2411-214: This framework has overcome two difficulties in graph-level representations learning under federated settings: 1) inaccessibility of graph-level ground-truth labels; 2) poor generalization performance of federated learning when different sites see distinct distributions (the non-IID issue).
elements.sent-2411-215: With a unique two-level contrastive learning mechanism, FCLG has attained high robustness even with non-IID and obtained high quality graph-level representations.
elements.sent-2411-216: Our extensive evaluation has shown FCLG achieved state-of-the-art graph-level clustering accuracy compared with the baselines we constructed, specifically, 2-7\% clustering accuracy gain in non-IID settings and 2-4\% in IID settings.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
